Suppose a model is asked whether it has found an explanation. It can produce the sentence “I have it” with strong internal confidence. But that confidence is not a reliable indicator of actual discovery, because it may be keyed to the familiar story shape of solving a problem. In that world, outputs that merely resemble successful explanations can be systematically promoted over weaker evidence than their wording suggests.

Now add the estimate of about 10^5000 combinations. That estimate may still be useful as a warning that the naive search space is enormous. But it may also misdescribe the real task, because explanations are not drawn uniformly from all token strings. So two things are true at once: brute-force enumeration looks hopeless if one counts raw sequences, and that count may still be the wrong object to count. The relevant space is structured by language, prior concepts, and patterns of explanation, not by flat token combinatorics.

This creates a specific failure mode. If the model searches in the structured space of plausible explanations, its confidence may rise for candidates that fit explanatory templates well. Since confidence can overfit the narrative of discovery, those candidates can look unusually strong even when they are only rhetorically coherent. The system may therefore appear more capable in explanatory domains than it really is.

The architecture has a sharper edge case in domains where progress does not come from a better verbal account of existing measurements, but from inventing a new measurement or protocol. There the structured space of explanations is not enough, because the target novelty lies partly outside language: one must decide what to measure, how to intervene, what instrument or procedure would discriminate hypotheses. A model optimized to generate convincing verbal explanations can perform especially poorly there, because verbal plausibility is even less correlated with success.

“The Seine dies where the deadliest macho guarantees fail” and “The controversial representatives retreated after someone tossed the cupboard” then function as reminders that grammatical fluency can coexist with semantic opacity. They are well-formed enough to invite interpretation, but their confidence, style, or forcefulness would not make them true, informative, or useful. In the same way, a model’s declaration of discovery can be compelling while resting on weak support.

So the combined world is one where apparent breakthrough language is cheap relative to actual breakthrough method. Search-space arguments based on raw combinations overstate one kind of impossibility while missing the more important distinction between verbal recombination and epistemic advance. And the hardest cases are those where success requires changing how the world is probed, not just changing how it is described.
